title: Recovering Sparse Neural Connectivity from Partial Measurements: A Covariance-Based Approach with Granger-Causality Refinement
core_question: Can we recover the connectivity matrix of a neural circuit from sparse, partial measurements across multiple recording sessions?
core_claim: Covariance accumulation across partial-observation sessions recovers sparse connectivity. Key findings: (1) the linear approximation provides implicit regularization via Price's theorem, (2) stimulation-measurement interaction determines optimal experimental protocol, (3) CPG-state correlation is the dominant error source and CPG nodes are detectable.
framing: General method for small brain circuits (C. elegans, zebrafish, organoids as examples, not focus)

hypotheses.statement: Covariance estimator recovers W better than chance
hypotheses.result: 82% improvement over chance at N=12, 66% measurement
Zero stim fails at high measurement; moderate stim (σ≈0.5) optimal

hypotheses.statement: Linear approximation degrades gracefully with nonlinearity mismatch
hypotheses.result: tanh BEST (bounded states → good κ), identity worse (unbounded drift)

hypotheses.statement: Approximate estimator beats oracle at all operating regimes
hypotheses.result: Oracle 2.9-10.4x worse across all stim levels

hypotheses.statement: Recovery improves with sensor coverage (number of stimulated neurons)
hypotheses.result: 1 sensor: 0.244 error; plateaus at 33%+ (~0.10). Sensor fraction controls rank of stimulation covariance.

key_contributions: Covariance accumulation framework for partial observations with identifiability theorem
Implicit regularization: linear approximation beats oracle (Price's theorem characterization)
Stimulation-measurement interaction (corrected finding: moderate stim needed at high measurement)
CPG-state correlation as dominant error source + variance-based CPG detection
